prompt: The palmerpenguins dataframe can be made available in R by calling `library(palmerpenguins)`. The dataframe is named `penguins`. It has the following factor columns: species, island, sex. It has the following numeric columns: bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, year. The palmerpenguins and ggplot2 libraries have been loaded for you. Construct a scatter plot with bill_length_mm on the x-axis and bill_depth_mm on the y-axis, coloured by species. Add an annotate() layer that places the text "Overlap region" at x = 45, y = 17 in size 4 font and colour "darkred". Do not store the plot in a variable. Respond with code only; no explanation. Do not re-iterate your objective or state anything about your reasoning process.
answer: ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, colour = species)) + geom_point() + annotate("text", x = 45, y = 17, label = "Overlap region", size = 4, colour = "darkred")

prompt: The palmerpenguins dataframe can be made available in R by calling `library(palmerpenguins)`. The dataframe is named `penguins`. It has the following factor columns: species, island, sex. It has the following numeric columns: bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, year. The palmerpenguins and ggplot2 libraries have been loaded for you. Construct a scatter plot with bill_length_mm on the x-axis and bill_depth_mm on the y-axis, coloured by species. Add an annotate() layer that draws an arrow segment from x = 50, y = 20 to xend = 47, yend = 18, with colour "black", and arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm")). Do not store the plot in a variable. Respond with code only; no explanation. Do not re-iterate your objective or state anything about your reasoning process.
answer: ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, colour = species)) + geom_point() + annotate("segment", x = 50, xend = 47, y = 20, yend = 18, colour = "black", arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm")))

prompt: The palmerpenguins dataframe can be made available in R by calling `library(palmerpenguins)`. The dataframe is named `penguins`. It has the following factor columns: species, island, sex. It has the following numeric columns: bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, year. The palmerpenguins and ggplot2 libraries have been loaded for you. Construct a pie chart of species counts by starting with a stacked bar chart (geom_bar(), x = "", fill = species), then transforming it to polar coordinates using coord_polar() with theta = "y". Do not store the plot in a variable. Respond with code only; no explanation. Do not re-iterate your objective or state anything about your reasoning process.
answer: ggplot(penguins, aes(x = "", fill = species)) + geom_bar() + coord_polar(theta = "y")

prompt: The palmerpenguins dataframe can be made available in R by calling `library(palmerpenguins)`. The dataframe is named `penguins`. It has the following factor columns: species, island, sex. It has the following numeric columns: bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, year. The palmerpenguins and ggplot2 libraries have been loaded for you. Construct a pie chart of species counts by starting with a stacked bar chart (geom_bar(), x = "", fill = species), then transforming it to polar coordinates using coord_polar() with theta = "y". Manually set the fill colours so that Adelie is "#E69F00", Chinstrap is "#56B4E9", and Gentoo is "#009E73". Apply theme_void(). Do not store the plot in a variable. Respond with code only; no explanation. Do not re-iterate your objective or state anything about your reasoning process.
answer: ggplot(penguins, aes(x = "", fill = species)) + geom_bar() + coord_polar(theta = "y") + scale_fill_manual(values = c(Adelie = "#E69F00", Chinstrap = "#56B4E9", Gentoo = "#009E73")) + theme_void()

prompt: The palmerpenguins dataframe can be made available in R by calling `library(palmerpenguins)`. The dataframe is named `penguins`. It has the following factor columns: species, island, sex. It has the following numeric columns: bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, year. The palmerpenguins and ggplot2 libraries have been loaded for you. Construct a boxplot with species on the x-axis and bill_length_mm on the y-axis. Using theme(), set the axis text size to 14 and the axis title size to 16. Do not store the plot in a variable. Respond with code only; no explanation. Do not re-iterate your objective or state anything about your reasoning process.
answer: ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = bill_length_mm)) + geom_boxplot() + theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 14), axis.title = element_text(size = 16))

prompt: The palmerpenguins dataframe can be made available in R by calling `library(palmerpenguins)`. The dataframe is named `penguins`. It has the following factor columns: species, island, sex. It has the following numeric columns: bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, year. The palmerpenguins and ggplot2 libraries have been loaded for you. Construct a scatter plot with flipper_length_mm on the x-axis and body_mass_g on the y-axis, coloured by species. Add the title "Flipper Length vs Body Mass". Using theme(), set the plot title font size to 18 and the legend title font size to 12. Do not store the plot in a variable. Respond with code only; no explanation. Do not re-iterate your objective or state anything about your reasoning process.
answer: ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, colour = species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Flipper Length vs Body Mass") + theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 18), legend.title = element_text(size = 12))

prompt: The palmerpenguins dataframe can be made available in R by calling `library(palmerpenguins)`. The dataframe is named `penguins`. It has the following factor columns: species, island, sex. It has the following numeric columns: bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, year. The palmerpenguins and ggplot2 libraries have been loaded for you. A summary dataframe named `mean_bill` has already been created for you with two columns: species and mean_bill_length (the mean bill_length_mm per species). Using `mean_bill`, construct a bar chart with species on the x-axis and mean_bill_length on the y-axis using geom_bar() with stat = "identity", filled by species. Manually set the fill colours so that Adelie is "#E69F00", Chinstrap is "#56B4E9", and Gentoo is "#009E73". Add a geom_text() layer showing the rounded mean_bill_length value (rounded to 1 decimal place) above each bar, nudged 0.5 units upward using position_nudge(). Set the axis title font sizes to 14 using theme(). Do not store the plot in a variable. Respond with code only; no explanation. Do not re-iterate your objective or state anything about your reasoning process.
answer: ggplot(mean_bill, aes(x = species, y = mean_bill_length, fill = species)) + geom_bar(stat = "identity") + scale_fill_manual(values = c(Adelie = "#E69F00", Chinstrap = "#56B4E9", Gentoo = "#009E73")) + geom_text(aes(label = round(mean_bill_length, 1)), position = position_nudge(y = 0.5)) + theme(axis.title = element_text(size = 14))
